claim: TCR/CD3 microdomains are a required to induce the immunologic synapse to activate T cells.

result.claim: TCR/CD3 microdomains are a required to induce the immunologic synapse to activate T cells.
result.reasoning:
The claim is strongly supported by the evidence. Multiple propositions and chunks indicate that TCR/CD3 clustering, conformational changes, and the formation of microclusters and the central supramolecular activation cluster (cSMAC), which is part of the immunological synapse, are essential for T cell activation.

Specifically:
*   Paper ID 33499189 (propositions prop_369, prop_373, prop_370, prop_371, and chunk_454) highlights that TCR-CD3 triggering involves receptor clustering and conformational changes at the CD3 subunits, and that these conformational changes are required for T cell activation. It also states that the induction of these changes requires multivalent engagement and mobility restriction of the TCR-CD3 complex, and is elicited by cooperative rearrangements of TCR-CD3 complexes. This directly supports the necessity of TCR/CD3 organization.
*   Paper ID 9283422 (proposition prop_889 and chunk_146) indicates that TCR signaling is initiated and sustained in microclusters, and that the cSMAC (a key component of the immunological synapse) is formed by the fusion of these microclusters. This demonstrates the role of TCR microdomains in initiating and sustaining T cell signaling.
*   Paper ID 306006 (proposition prop_1271 and chunk_4) states that T cell activation is predicated on the interaction between the T cell receptor and peptide-major histocompatibility ligands, and that the stimulatory potency of T cell antigens is influenced by the formation of the immunological synapse. It also suggests that the inability to form the cSMAC could impact T cell proliferation. This directly links the immunological synapse formation to T cell activation.

While the papers currently show "Year: Unknown" and "Citations: 0", the consistent and direct nature of the evidence across these different sources provides strong support for the claim.

evidence.text: Mouse studies indicate that tissue localization of T cell subsets is important for both protective immunity and immunoregulation.
evidence.source: Early life compartmentalization of human T cell differentiation and regulatory function in mucosal and lymphoid tissues

evidence.text: MDSCs directly disrupt the binding of specific peptide–major histocompatibility complex (pMHC) dimers to CD8-expressing T cells through nitration of tyrosines in a T-cell receptor (TCR)-CD8 complex.
evidence.source: Altered recognition of antigen is a mechanism of CD8+ T cell tolerance in cancer

evidence.text: Effector memory T cells (T(EM)) were found only in the lungs and small intestine of pediatric subjects.
evidence.source: Early life compartmentalization of human T cell differentiation and regulatory function in mucosal and lymphoid tissues

evidence.text: In adult tissues the main subset of T cells are memory T cells.
evidence.source: Early life compartmentalization of human T cell differentiation and regulatory function in mucosal and lymphoid tissues

evidence.text: Memory CD4+ T cell–induced IICs and viral control are optimal when memory cells are either T helper type 1 (TH1) or TH17 polarized.
evidence.source: Memory CD4+ T cells induce innate responses independently of pathogen

evidence.text: T cell activation is predicated on the interaction between the T cell receptor and peptide-major histocompatibility ligands.
evidence.source: The stimulatory potency of T cell antigens is influenced by the formation of the immunological synapse.

evidence.text: The conformational change at CD3 is required for T cell activation.
evidence.source: Full activation of the T cell receptor requires both clustering and conformational changes at CD3.

evidence.text: Treg cells lacking expression of integrin αvβ8 were unable to suppress pathogenic T cell responses during active inflammation.
evidence.source: Integrin αvβ8-Mediated TGF-β Activation by Effector Regulatory T Cells Is Essential for Suppression of T-Cell-Mediated Inflammation

evidence.text: Triggering of PD-1 expressed on monocytes by PD-L1 led to reversible CD4+ T cell dysfunction.
evidence.source: Programmed death-1–induced interleukin-10 production by monocytes impairs CD4+ T cell activation during HIV infection

evidence.text: Transcriptional activation of p53-S100A8/9-TLR signaling in the mesenchymal niche predicted leukemic evolution in myelodysplastic syndrome.
evidence.source: Mesenchymal Inflammation Drives Genotoxic Stress in Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Predicts Disease Evolution in Human Pre-leukemia.

evidence.text: B cells interact with helper T cells during humoral immune responses.
evidence.source: Finding the right niche: B-cell migration in the early phases of T-dependent antibody responses.

evidence.text: Nitration of TCR-CD8 is induced by MDSCs through hyperproduction of reactive oxygen species and peroxynitrite during direct cell-cell contact.
evidence.source: Altered recognition of antigen is a mechanism of CD8+ T cell tolerance in cancer

evidence.text: T-zone chemokines guide activated B cells to the T zone.
evidence.source: Finding the right niche: B-cell migration in the early phases of T-dependent antibody responses.

evidence.text: A fraction of CD4(+) T cells express the CX3CL1 receptor, CX3CR1, in people with asthma.
evidence.source: CX3CR1 is required for airway inflammation by promoting T helper cell survival and maintenance in inflamed lung

evidence.text: The central supramolecular activation cluster (cSMAC) formed by fusion of microclusters contained more CD45 than microclusters.
evidence.source: T cell receptor-proximal signals are sustained in peripheral microclusters and terminated in the central supramolecular activation cluster.

evidence.text: T cell receptor (TCR-CD3) triggering involves both receptor clustering and conformational changes at the cytoplasmic tails of the CD3 subunits.
evidence.source: Full activation of the T cell receptor requires both clustering and conformational changes at CD3.

evidence.text: Transcription factor GATA-3 is known to be vital for the development of T cells at multiple stages in the thymus and for Th2 differentiation in the peripheral organs.
evidence.source: GATA-3 regulates hematopoietic stem cell maintenance and cell-cycle entry.

evidence.text: The T helper 1 (Th1) cell cytokine IFN-gamma induces autophagy in macrophages to eliminate Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
evidence.source: T helper 2 cytokines inhibit autophagic control of intracellular Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

evidence.text: Memory CD4+ T cells enhance production of multiple innate inflammatory cytokines and chemokines (IICs) in the lung.
evidence.source: Memory CD4+ T cells induce innate responses independently of pathogen

evidence.text: Antigen-specific CD8+ T-cell tolerance is induced by myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs).
evidence.source: Altered recognition of antigen is a mechanism of CD8+ T cell tolerance in cancer

evidence.text: Blocking interactions between IL-10 and IL-10 receptor (IL-10R) improves T cell function.
evidence.source: Programmed death-1–induced interleukin-10 production by monocytes impairs CD4+ T cell activation during HIV infection

evidence.text: Monocytes, macrophages, and dendritic cells are mononuclear phagocytes.
evidence.source: Fate mapping reveals origins and dynamics of monocytes and tissue macrophages under homeostasis.

evidence.text: Induction of the conformational change requires both multivalent engagement and the mobility restriction of the TCR-CD3 imposed by the plasma membrane.
evidence.source: Full activation of the T cell receptor requires both clustering and conformational changes at CD3.

evidence.text: TNF-α produced by CD103(-)CD11b(+) dendritic cells synergized with IL-23 to drive IL-17A production by ILCs.
evidence.source: The Transcription Factor T-bet Regulates Intestinal Inflammation Mediated by Interleukin-7 Receptor+ Innate Lymphoid Cells

evidence.text: The conformational change is elicited by cooperative rearrangements of two TCR-CD3 complexes.
evidence.source: Full activation of the T cell receptor requires both clustering and conformational changes at CD3.
